findings.description: The scout's report in Section 1 delivers political aftermath (Conclave in emergency session, new hedge-Shapers, Thalen's custody) that, while necessary for the reader's sense of resolution, reads as slightly expository in what is otherwise the book's most emotionally present chapter. The report is efficient, but it momentarily turns Lirien into a receiver of briefing updates rather than a woman waking up in a changed world.
findings.readerImpact: Mild tonal disruption. The chapter opens with Lirien's gorgeous sensory inventory of the changed world, then pauses for a status briefing, then returns to emotional territory. The middle section is the least engaging part of an otherwise excellent chapter.

findings.description: The Conclave's institutional non-apology ('circumstances surrounding the Calwrath disciplinary action remain under review') is realistically infuriating and Kaelith's response ('I stopped needing their permission when I started needing something real') is the perfect landing for her institutional-trust arc. The risk is that some readers may feel unsatisfied that Calwrath is never formally resolved — no vindication, no public reversal. But this IS the point: the resolution is Kaelith not needing institutional validation anymore.
findings.location: Section 2, Conclave relay and Kaelith's response
findings.readerImpact: Readers who want justice-as-vindication will feel the lack. Readers who have tracked Kaelith's arc will recognize this as the deeper resolution — not the institution changing, but Kaelith no longer needing it to. The HFN promise depends on whether the reader trusts this as sufficient.

findings.description: The physical intimacy in the resolution is extremely restrained — hand-holding, sitting together, thumb tracing knuckles. There is no kiss, no embrace, no explicit physical reunion beyond the foreheads-touching at the end of Ch 20. For a romance novel, this is a significant choice. It's the RIGHT choice for these characters (both are physically reserved, both express love through presence and action rather than display), but romance readers expecting a traditional resolution scene may feel the ending is emotionally satisfying but physically insufficient.
findings.location: Final scene, hand-holding against the shelter wall
findings.readerImpact: This will be the most divisive element of the ending. The restraint is thematically perfect (the love is proven through staying, not through physical demonstration) but some readers will actively miss a more physical reunion. The thumb across knuckles IS the kiss for these characters — the question is whether enough readers will read it that way.

findings.description: The river metaphor in the final paragraphs is the book's most extended metaphorical passage and serves as Lirien's thematic thesis statement. The distinction between 'flowing' (following gravity) and 'deciding' (a dammed river choosing its own course) perfectly captures the contract-vs-free-will argument that has driven the entire romance. The metaphor earns its space because it has been building all book (water imagery, 'the Seep,' current metaphors throughout). This is the payoff.
findings.location: Final paragraphs, river metaphor through 'That was deciding.'
findings.readerImpact: Deeply satisfying for readers who have been tracking the water/current imagery. The metaphor lands because it's been earned across 22 chapters. 'That was deciding' is the thematic capstone of the book.

strengths: The quiet of 'after' is rendered with extraordinary precision. The chapter understands that the hardest part of a crisis story is what comes when the crisis is over. 'The quiet of after, which was larger and stranger than any crisis had been' — this is exactly right.

'I stay because I want to' is the perfect mirror of the contract. The entire book has been asking whether desire can exist independently of compulsion. In the quiet, with no mission and no crisis and no binding, Lirien answers: yes. Because I want to. The simplicity is devastating after 22 chapters of analytical complexity.

Kaelith's future vision (fixing Rivenmarch's fortifications, correcting the watch positions) is perfect characterization — of course her idea of happiness is making walls better. The gentle humor of it, and Lirien's affectionate recognition, is the book's warmest moment.

The HFN works. It works because 'happy for now' is exactly what these characters can honestly offer. Not a permanent guarantee — a daily choice. 'Tomorrow I will choose it again.' The ending doesn't oversell. It promises exactly as much as it can deliver.

The star through the thinned Pallor is the perfect final image — real light through a damaged-but-holding membrane. The world is harder now. Less protected. More honest. The star is real. That's enough.

Lirien's identity question ('Who is Lirien Dael, when no one is telling her what to do?') is left open, which is exactly right for an HFN ending and for a potential series. She's building the answer. She hasn't finished building it.
